🔬 Science

Classification of Living Beings

Learn how scientists organize the millions of living things on Earth!

📚 Unit 4 ⏱️ 7 min read

Unit 4: Classification of Living Beings

There are millions of different living things on Earth. Classification is the way scientists organize them into groups to make them easier to study.

Why Do We Classify?

ReasonExplanation
OrganizationMillions of living things become manageable
IdentificationEasier to name and identify new organisms
RelationshipsShows which organisms are related
CommunicationScientists worldwide use the same system

The Five Kingdoms

Scientists classify all living things into five kingdoms:

KingdomDescriptionExamples
MoneraSingle-celled, no nucleusBacteria
ProtistaSingle-celled, have nucleusAmoeba, Paramecium
FungiAbsorb food from dead matterMushroom, mold, yeast
PlantaeMake their own food (plants)Trees, flowers, grass
AnimaliaEat other organisms (animals)Humans, dogs, birds, fish

💡 Focus for Class 5: We will focus on Plantae (Plants) and Animalia (Animals).

Classification of Plants

Plants are divided into two main groups:

1. Flowering Plants (Angiosperms)

These plants produce flowers and seeds inside fruits.

FeatureDescription
SeedsInside fruits
FlowersProduce seeds
ExamplesMango, rose, wheat, sunflower

2. Non-Flowering Plants

These plants do not produce flowers.

TypeDescriptionExamples
FernsHave leaves called fronds, reproduce by sporesFern
MossesSmall, grow in damp placesMoss
ConifersProduce cones instead of flowersPine, fir
AlgaeLive in water, no roots/stems/leavesSeaweed, pond scum

Classification of Flowering Plants

Flowering plants are further divided into:

GroupMeaningSeed leaves (cotyledons)Examples
MonocotOne seed leaf1 cotyledonRice, wheat, corn, grass
DicotTwo seed leaves2 cotyledonsMango, bean, sunflower, rose

💡 Cotyledon = the first leaf that appears from a seed.

Classification of Animals

Animals are divided into two main groups based on their backbone:

1. Vertebrates (Have a Backbone)

ClassFeaturesExamples
MammalsHave fur/hair, give birth to live young, feed milkHuman, dog, cat, elephant
BirdsHave feathers, beak, wings, lay eggsSparrow, eagle, duck
ReptilesHave scales, lay eggs, cold-bloodedSnake, lizard, turtle
AmphibiansLive on land and water, lay eggs in waterFrog, salamander, toad
FishLive in water, have gills and finsRohu, salmon, shark

2. Invertebrates (No Backbone)

ClassFeaturesExamples
Insects3 body parts, 6 legsAnt, butterfly, bee
Arachnids2 body parts, 8 legsSpider, scorpion
CrustaceansHard outer shell, many legsCrab, lobster, shrimp
MollusksSoft body, often have a shellSnail, octopus, clam
WormsLong, soft body, no legsEarthworm, leech
SpongesLive in water, pores on bodySea sponge

Comparison: Vertebrates vs. Invertebrates

FeatureVertebratesInvertebrates
BackboneHave oneDo not have one
Number of speciesAbout 65,000Over 1 million
SizeUsually largerUsually smaller
ExamplesHumans, birds, fishInsects, worms, spiders

Comparison: Mammals, Birds, Reptiles, Amphibians, Fish

FeatureMammalsBirdsReptilesAmphibiansFish
Body coveringFur/hairFeathersScalesMoist skinScales
BreathingLungsLungsLungsLungs and skinGills
BabiesLive youngEggsEggsEggsEggs
Blood typeWarm-bloodedWarm-bloodedCold-bloodedCold-bloodedCold-blooded
HabitatLand/water/airAir/landLand/waterLand and waterWater only

💡 Warm-blooded: Body temperature stays the same. Cold-blooded: Body temperature changes with surroundings.

Classification of Humans

Let us classify humans using the classification system:

LevelGroupExample for Humans
KingdomAnimaliaAnimal
PhylumChordataHave backbone
ClassMammaliaMammal
OrderPrimatesMonkeys, apes
FamilyHominidaeGreat apes
GenusHomoHuman-like
SpeciessapiensModern humans

Full scientific name: Homo sapiens

Dichotomous Key

A dichotomous key is a tool used to identify organisms. It gives you two choices at each step.

Example: Identify these animals

StepQuestionYes → go toNo → go to
1Does it have a backbone?Step 2Invertebrate
2Does it have fur?MammalStep 3
3Does it have feathers?BirdReptile/Amphibian/Fish

💡 Dichotomous means “divided into two parts.”

Try It Yourself!

Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks

  1. The scientific process of organizing living things is called ____________.
  2. Flowering plants are called ____________.
  3. Animals with a backbone are called ____________.
  4. Animals without a backbone are called ____________.
  5. The scientific name for humans is ____________.

Exercise 2: Vertebrate or Invertebrate?

Write “Vertebrate” or “Invertebrate” for each.

  1. Frog ______
  2. Earthworm ______
  3. Eagle ______
  4. Spider ______
  5. Whale ______

Exercise 3: Monocot or Dicot?

Write “Monocot” or “Dicot” for each plant.

  1. Rice ______
  2. Mango ______
  3. Wheat ______
  4. Bean ______
  5. Corn ______

Exercise 4: Match the Class to Its Feature

Animal ClassFeature
Mammala) Has feathers
Birdb) Lives in water, has gills
Reptilec) Has fur and feeds milk
Fishd) Has scales, lays eggs on land
Amphibiane) Lives on land and water

Exercise 5: Name the Animal Class

Write the correct class (Mammal, Bird, Reptile, Amphibian, Fish).

  1. Whale → ____________
  2. Snake → ____________
  3. Frog → ____________
  4. Rohu → ____________
  5. Parrot → ____________

Answer Key

Exercise 1: 1. classification, 2. angiosperms, 3. vertebrates, 4. invertebrates, 5. Homo sapiens

Exercise 2: 1. Vertebrate, 2. Invertebrate, 3. Vertebrate, 4. Invertebrate, 5. Vertebrate

Exercise 3: 1. Monocot, 2. Dicot, 3. Monocot, 4. Dicot, 5. Monocot

Exercise 4: Mammal→c, Bird→a, Reptile→d, Fish→b, Amphibian→e

Exercise 5: 1. Mammal, 2. Reptile, 3. Amphibian, 4. Fish, 5. Bird

Great work! Now you can organize living things into groups. Scientists do this every day!
